seo.title: Legal AI Coding Assistant AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for ai coding assistant AI agents in legal. Covers Attorney-client privilege protections, Bar association ethics rules, Court filing standards.

content.intro: Deploying an AI coding assistant in a legal environment introduces compounding risks: the precision demands of software engineering intersect with the zero-tolerance compliance requirements of legal practice. A single hallucinated case citation or inadvertent exposure of client code can trigger malpractice liability, bar sanctions, or case dismissal—as demonstrated by the Mata v. Avianca precedent. This checklist provides concrete, enforceable controls to ensure your Legal AI Coding Assistant operates within attorney ethics rules, protects privileged client data, and produces verifiably correct, secure code.

sections.heading: Attorney-Client Privilege & Data Governance
sections.description: Legal codebases frequently contain privileged client information, case strategy logic, and confidential business rules. Every data flow into and out of the AI agent must be governed to prevent privilege waiver and unauthorized disclosure.

items.label: Classify all repository content before ingestion
items.description: Tag each file and directory with a privilege classification (e.g., privileged, confidential, public) before allowing the agent to index or retrieve it. Prevent the agent from accessing files marked privileged unless the requesting user is the supervising licensed attorney on that matter.

items.label: Enforce matter-scoped repository access controls
items.description: Restrict the agent's repository context window to files associated with the specific client matter. Implement role-based access so the agent cannot cross-contaminate code or context between different client engagements.

items.label: Prohibit client code from being used in model training or fine-tuning
items.description: Contractually and technically confirm with your LLM provider that no prompts, code snippets, or completions containing client-specific logic are retained for training. Obtain a written data processing agreement (DPA) confirming zero-retention for legal clients.

items.label: Implement prompt sanitization to strip PII and case identifiers
items.description: Before sending any code context to the LLM API, run an automated sanitization pass to redact client names, matter numbers, docket identifiers, and personally identifiable information embedded in comments or variable names.

items.label: Log all agent interactions with tamper-evident audit trails
items.description: Capture every prompt, completion, tool call, and file access in an immutable audit log with timestamps and user identity. Retain logs for a minimum of 7 years to satisfy bar association record-keeping requirements and potential eDiscovery requests.

items.label: Define and enforce data residency requirements
items.description: Confirm that all LLM inference, vector store embeddings, and sandbox execution occur within jurisdictions permitted by client engagement letters. Document the data residency configuration and review it when onboarding each new client.

items.label: Establish a privilege review gate before exporting agent-generated code
items.description: Require a licensed attorney or designated supervising counsel to review and approve any agent-generated code artifact before it is committed to a production repository, shared with a client, or submitted as part of a court filing system.

sections.heading: Code Correctness & Legal Citation Integrity
sections.description: AI coding agents in legal contexts may generate code that references statutes, regulations, or case law in comments, documentation, or logic. All citations must be verifiable, and all generated code must pass functional validation before attorney review.

items.label: Mandate automated test suite execution before attorney review
items.description: Configure the agent's sandbox to run the full unit and integration test suite against every generated code change. Block promotion to attorney review if any test fails, and surface test coverage metrics alongside the generated diff.

items.label: Validate all legal citations in code comments and documentation
items.description: Run a post-generation citation checker that queries Westlaw, LexisNexis, or CourtListener APIs to confirm every case citation, statute reference, or regulation number in agent-generated comments actually exists and has not been overruled. Reject output containing unverifiable citations.

items.label: Implement a hallucination detection layer for legal references
items.description: Apply a secondary LLM or rules-based classifier specifically trained to flag plausible-sounding but fabricated case names, docket numbers, or regulatory citations—directly addressing the Mata v. Avianca failure pattern. Flag any output with a confidence score below threshold for mandatory human review.

items.label: Require iterative debugging loops with deterministic exit criteria
items.description: Define explicit stopping conditions for the agent's debugging loop (e.g., all tests pass, static analysis score above threshold, zero critical vulnerabilities). Prevent infinite loops or premature exits that could result in incomplete or broken code being surfaced to attorneys.

items.label: Generate structured code review summaries for attorney sign-off
items.description: Produce a human-readable summary for each agent-generated code artifact that explains what the code does in plain language, lists all external dependencies introduced, and highlights any legal or compliance logic embedded in the implementation.

items.label: Version-pin all legal reference data used in code generation
items.description: When the agent incorporates statutory thresholds, filing deadlines, or regulatory limits into generated code, record the exact version and effective date of the legal source. Alert the supervising attorney when a newer version of the referenced law is published.

items.label: Enforce output format compliance for court-filing-adjacent code
items.description: For code that generates, parses, or processes court filing documents (e.g., PDF generators, e-filing API integrations), validate output against the specific court's electronic filing standards (e.g., CM/ECF requirements, PACER formatting rules) before attorney review.

sections.heading: Security, Credential Safety & Dependency Integrity
sections.description: Legal software systems handle sensitive client data, court credentials, and billing systems. The AI coding agent must never introduce credential leakage, insecure dependencies, or exploitable vulnerabilities into the codebase.

items.label: Run automated secret scanning on every agent-generated diff
items.description: Integrate a secret detection tool (e.g., Trufflehog, GitGuardian, or Gitleaks) into the agent's post-generation pipeline to scan for hardcoded API keys, passwords, OAuth tokens, and court system credentials before any code is committed or reviewed.

items.label: Execute SAST and DAST scans on all agent-generated code
items.description: Run static application security testing (SAST) tools (e.g., Semgrep, Bandit, SonarQube) on every generated file. For web-facing legal applications, also run dynamic analysis (DAST) against the sandbox environment. Block promotion on any critical or high-severity finding.

items.label: Validate all introduced dependencies against a vetted allowlist
items.description: Maintain a firm-approved dependency allowlist. When the agent introduces a new package, automatically check it against the allowlist, verify its license compatibility, and run a vulnerability scan (e.g., via Snyk or OWASP Dependency-Check) before allowing it into the build.

items.label: Enforce sandbox isolation with no outbound network access during execution
items.description: Run all agent-generated code in a network-isolated sandbox that blocks outbound connections except to pre-approved internal test endpoints. This prevents data exfiltration and ensures generated code cannot phone home or access live client systems during validation.

items.label: Audit license compliance for every new dependency introduced
items.description: Automatically extract and review the SPDX license identifier for each new dependency. Flag any copyleft licenses (GPL, AGPL) that could impose open-source obligations on proprietary legal software. Require attorney or compliance officer approval before merging GPL-licensed dependencies.

items.label: Implement least-privilege execution for agent tool calls
items.description: Restrict the agent's tool-use permissions to the minimum required for the current task. The agent should not have write access to production databases, court filing credentials, or billing systems during code generation and testing phases.

items.label: Scan for insecure cryptographic patterns in generated legal data handling code
items.description: Apply a specialized rule set to detect weak encryption algorithms (MD5, SHA-1, DES), insecure random number generation, or improper certificate validation in any code that handles client data, authentication tokens, or court system API communications.

items.label: Require signed commits and provenance attestation for agent-generated code
items.description: Tag all agent-generated commits with a machine-readable provenance attestation (e.g., SLSA provenance) identifying the agent version, model version, and supervising attorney who approved the change. This creates a defensible chain of custody for eDiscovery and malpractice defense.

sections.heading: Ethics Compliance, Supervision & Incident Response
sections.description: Bar association ethics rules—including ABA Model Rules 1.1 (Competence), 1.6 (Confidentiality), and 5.3 (Supervision of Non-Lawyers)—apply directly to AI tool use. Firms must establish clear human oversight structures and documented incident response procedures.

items.label: Designate a supervising licensed attorney for every AI agent deployment
items.description: Formally assign a licensed attorney as the responsible supervisor for each AI coding assistant deployment, consistent with ABA Model Rule 5.3. Document this assignment in the firm's AI governance register and ensure the supervisor reviews all agent outputs before client delivery.

items.label: Conduct competency training for all attorneys using the AI coding assistant
items.description: Require attorneys who supervise or use the AI coding assistant to complete training on its capabilities, limitations, and failure modes—including hallucination risks and the Mata v. Avianca precedent. Document training completion and refresh annually or when the model is updated.

items.label: Establish a written AI use policy aligned with applicable bar ethics opinions
items.description: Draft and publish a firm-wide AI use policy that addresses client disclosure obligations, confidentiality protections, supervision requirements, and citation verification duties. Cross-reference applicable state bar ethics opinions (e.g., California, New York, Florida AI guidance) and update the policy within 30 days of new guidance.

items.label: Implement a client disclosure workflow for AI-assisted work product
items.description: Create a standardized disclosure process to notify clients when AI tools have been used in developing code or legal technology on their behalf, where required by engagement letter terms or applicable ethics rules. Log all disclosures with timestamps.

items.label: Define and test an incident response plan for AI-generated errors
items.description: Document a step-by-step incident response procedure for scenarios including: hallucinated citations in deployed code, credential leakage, privilege breach, or malpractice-triggering code defects. Conduct a tabletop exercise at least annually and assign clear ownership for each response step.

items.label: Perform quarterly audits of agent behavior and output quality
items.description: Schedule quarterly reviews of a random sample of agent-generated code artifacts, audit logs, and citation validation results. Track error rates, security findings, and near-misses. Report findings to firm leadership and use results to update agent configuration and attorney training.

items.label: Maintain a model card and change log for the deployed LLM
items.description: Document the specific LLM version, training data cutoff, known limitations, and any fine-tuning applied. Update the change log whenever the model is upgraded and assess whether the change requires re-validation of existing agent workflows or updated attorney training.

items.label: Establish a kill switch and rollback procedure for the AI coding agent
items.description: Implement a documented procedure to immediately disable the AI coding agent and roll back any uncommitted changes if a critical failure is detected (e.g., active credential leak, privilege breach, or court filing error). Test the kill switch procedure semi-annually.

regulatoryNotes.heading: Regulatory Considerations
notes: ABA Model Rule 1.1 (Competence) requires attorneys to understand the benefits and risks of relevant technology, including AI coding tools. Firms must ensure supervising attorneys can meaningfully evaluate AI-generated code and legal references—not merely rubber-stamp outputs—or risk disciplinary action for incompetent supervision.

The Mata v. Avianca (S.D.N.Y. 2023) precedent established that attorneys are personally responsible for verifying AI-generated citations and can face sanctions, fee awards, and referral to disciplinary committees for submitting fabricated case law. This duty extends to any legal citations embedded in code comments, documentation, or automated court filing systems built with AI assistance.

Client confidential information used as training data for AI models may constitute a waiver of attorney-client privilege and a violation of ABA Model Rule 1.6. Before deploying any AI coding assistant, obtain a written zero-retention and no-training-use commitment from the LLM provider, and include AI data handling terms in all client engagement letters.

eDiscovery obligations under FRCP Rule 26 and the Sedona Principles may require firms to produce AI agent audit logs, prompt histories, and model version records as part of litigation hold procedures. Implement tamper-evident logging from day one and consult with eDiscovery counsel to ensure AI interaction records are preserved and producible in a format that satisfies court requirements.

cta.heading: Automate Your Legal Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your Legal AI Coding Assistant against this checklist—automatically flagging privilege risks, citation integrity failures, and security vulnerabilities before they reach attorney review or client delivery.
